numbers.meaning: Divine completeness; the divine triad pattern
numbers.note: Three denials, three days, three temptations — structural completeness. Three is associated with divine action and completion in ANE thought.

numbers.meaning: Earthly completeness; the four corners of the world
numbers.note: Four winds, four living creatures, four directions — represents the whole of the created order. Four beasts in Daniel = four world empires = the totality of Gentile rule.

numbers.meaning: Incompleteness; one short of the divine seven
numbers.note: 666 in Revelation = triple incompleteness, emphatic failure to achieve divine perfection. Numbers three short of 7 signal falling short of wholeness.
Seven-day week; seven seals/trumpets/bowls in Revelation; seventy weeks in Daniel; forgive 77 times = without limit. The most pervasive biblical number.

numbers.meaning: Human completeness; fullness of a human scale
numbers.note: Ten commandments; ten plagues; tithe (one-tenth). Ten is the fingers of two hands — a complete counting.
Twelve tribes; twelve apostles; twelve foundation stones in the New Jerusalem; twelve baskets. Signals complete covenant community.

numbers.meaning: Testing and transition; a complete period of trial
numbers.note: Forty years wandering; forty days of flood; forty days of Jesus's temptation; forty years of Saul/David/Solomon reigns. A generation of trial.
Seventy nations in Gen 10 table; seventy elders of Israel (Exod 24:1); seventy weeks of Daniel (9:24); Jesus sends out 70 disciples (Luke 10:1). Seven times ten = complete international scope.

numbers.meaning: The complete people of God from all Israel (12 × 12 × 1000)
numbers.note: Rev 7:4 and 14:1. Not a literal number — it is 12 tribes × 12 apostles × 1000 (the number of fullness). Represents the total complete people of God across both covenants.

'A thousand generations' = forever; the millennium (Rev 20) = a complete period of divine rule; 'cattle on a thousand hills' (Ps 50:10) = all cattle belong to God. A round number of immense magnitude.

colors.meaning: Purity, divine holiness, victory, and eschatological glory
colors.note: White garments in Revelation = the righteousness of the saints (Rev 19:8). The Ancient of Days' garment is white (Dan 7:9). The transfiguration (Mark 9:3). White is the color of heaven.

'Though your sins are like scarlet' (Isa 1:18). The scarlet thread (Josh 2:18) — salvation through blood. Scarlet robe of the soldiers (Matt 27:28). The great whore's scarlet (Rev 17:4) = imperial Rome.

Purple was the most expensive dye in the ancient world — extracted from sea mollusks, it marked the highest status. The Praetorian soldiers' mockery (Mark 15:17). The rich man's purple (Luke 16:19). Lydia sold purple cloth (Acts 16:14).

colors.meaning: Divine glory, highest value, and permanence
colors.note: The New Jerusalem's gold (Rev 21:18) — the material of heaven. Gold refined by fire (1 Pet 1:7) = faith proved genuine. The golden lampstand (tabernacle) — God's presence.
The black horse of Revelation 6:5 = famine. Amos 8:9 — the sun going dark at noon. Darkness and blackness are associated with divine absence and judgment throughout.

colors.meaning: Life, growth, and prosperity (sometimes death in Rev)
colors.note: The green tree (Luke 23:31) vs. dry tree = life vs. death. The pale green (χλωρός) horse of Rev 6:8 = death — the color of corpse pallor.

spatial.meaning: Place of divine encounter, revelation, and rule
spatial.note: Sinai/Horeb = the mountain of the Law. Zion = God's holy mountain, his throne. The Sermon on the Mount echoes Sinai. The mount of Transfiguration. The New Jerusalem descends as a mountain (Rev 21:10).

spatial.meaning: Paradise, the original created order, and the presence of God
spatial.note: Eden = place of God's presence with humanity (Gen 2). Gethsemane = a garden (John 18:1). The New Jerusalem has the river of life and the tree of life — Eden restored (Rev 22:1–5).

spatial.meaning: Place of testing, purification, and divine provision
spatial.note: Israel's forty years in the wilderness. Elijah's wilderness journey. John the Baptist in the wilderness. Jesus tempted in the wilderness. The wilderness is where God tests and forms his people.

spatial.meaning: Chaos, death, and the anti-creation powers
spatial.note: The primordial deep (תְּהוֹם, Gen 1:2). The sea in Revelation = the source of the Beast (13:1). Jesus stilling the sea = mastering chaos. 'No more sea' in Rev 21:1 = no more chaos and death.

spatial.meaning: Sacred site for worship — legitimate and illegitimate
spatial.note: High places (בָּמוֹת) were ANE worship sites on hilltops. Before the temple, Israel used them legitimately (1 Sam 9:12). After Sinai, they became sites of syncretism (2 Kgs 17:9–12) — the primary accusation against apostate kings.

spatial.meaning: Human civilization organized without God (Babylon) vs. with God (Jerusalem)
spatial.note: The two cities of Revelation: Babylon (Rome/the world system opposed to God) and the New Jerusalem (the bride of Christ). The Cain-Abel contrast parallels city vs. wandering.

spatial.meaning: The direction of God's presence; also exile
spatial.note: The gate of Eden facing east (Gen 3:24). The Shekinah glory departed eastward in Ezekiel (Ezek 10:19; 11:23). The glory returns from the east (Ezek 43:2). Exile is going east (into Babylon).

spatial.meaning: Humiliation, death, the realm of the dead
spatial.note: The descent to Sheol = death. Jesus's descent in Phil 2:8 and Eph 4:9. The descent to the pit. Descending represents humiliation, powerlessness, death.

cosmological.meaning: The most fundamental binary: God's ordered creation vs. chaos/sin/evil
cosmological.note: Creation begins with 'Let there be light' (Gen 1:3). God is light, in him is no darkness (1 John 1:5). Jesus is the light of the world (John 8:12). Darkness = the realm of the enemy (Col 1:13).

cosmological.meaning: Revelation / hiddenness; salvation / judgment; activity / passivity
cosmological.note: 'Now is the acceptable time, now is the day of salvation' (2 Cor 6:2). The 'day of the LORD' = the day of divine judgment. 'The night is far gone, the day is at hand' (Rom 13:12). Eschatological urgency.

Stars falling from heaven (Isa 14:12; Rev 12:4) = angelic/spiritual powers being judged. The 'morning star' (Rev 22:16) = Jesus as supreme heavenly being. The seven stars in Jesus's hand (Rev 1:16) = seven angels of seven churches.

cosmological.meaning: The divine presence and power in action (same word in Hebrew: רוּחַ)
cosmological.note: The Spirit of God hovering over the deep (Gen 1:2). The breath of life (Gen 2:7). Ezekiel's valley of dry bones (Ezek 37:9–10). 'The wind blows where it wishes' (John 3:8). Pentecost's rushing wind (Acts 2:2).

cosmological.meaning: Divine presence, judgment, purification, and theophany
cosmological.note: The burning bush (Exod 3:2). The pillar of fire (Exod 13:21). Elijah's fire from heaven (1 Kgs 18:38). Pentecost's tongues of fire (Acts 2:3). 'Our God is a consuming fire' (Heb 12:29).

cosmological.meaning: Cleansing, chaos, new creation, and the Spirit
cosmological.note: Waters of creation (Gen 1:2). Waters of the flood (chaos). Waters of the Red Sea (death and new life). Baptism = death and resurrection. 'Rivers of living water' (John 7:38–39) = the Spirit. No more sea in the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:1).

cosmological.meaning: The divine presence concealed and revealed
cosmological.note: God appears in the cloud on Sinai (Exod 19:9). The cloud fills the tabernacle (Exod 40:34). The cloud at the Transfiguration (Mark 9:7). Jesus's ascension in a cloud (Acts 1:9). The Son of Man 'coming on the clouds' (Dan 7:13; Mark 14:62).
